coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as an antimicrobial agent against Staphylococcus for the treatment of infected wounds
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chengfei
Wang, Xuewen
Wu, Lina
Wu, Wen
Zheng, Yanjie
Lin, Liqing
Weng, Shaohuang
Lin, Xinhua
coredata.dc:description: Antimicrobial resistance is becoming more and more serious and has become a potential hazard to human life and health. The fabrication of some new antibacterial substances against resistant bacteria is demanded. With the wide application and research of carbon nanomaterials, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized by a one-step chemical route herein. The particle size of NCQDs in the range of 2–5 nm were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy, and dynamic light scattering. The functional groups and optical properties of NCQDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Disk-diffusion tests showed that the NCQDs had specific antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus. TEM showed that the NCQDs could destroy the cell structure of Staphylococcus aureus and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) but could not combat Escherichia coli. The antibacterial mechanism may be that positively charged NCQDs firstly interacted with the negatively charged bacteria, and then specifically anchored on some specific sites on the surface of Staphylococcus. The NCQDs were applied to treat wounds infected with MRSA and showed the same therapeutic effect as vancomycin. Photomicrographs of hematoxylin-eosin-stained histological sections showed that the NCQDs at concentrations effectively killing S. aureus and MRSA caused negligible toxicity to the main rat organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. Thus, the NCQDs can be developed as a promising antibacterial agent for Staphylococcus. And the NCQDs are likely to treat local infections caused by Staphylococcus clinically, especially S. aureus and MRSA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Staphylococcus
Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
Escherichia coli
Infected wounds

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: TEM characterization of NCQDs against bacteria
Viability of HeLa and PANC-1 cells
Antibacterial activity of NCQDs in vitro
Therapeutic effect of NCQDs on infected wound in vivo
Toxicity test of NCQDs in vitro and in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and antibacterial properties of NCQDs and their therapeutic effect on wounds infected with MRSA.
TEM image (A), HRTEM image (B) and AFM image (C) of the prepared NCQDs; height profile along the lines of AFM (D).
XPS survey spectrum (A), and deconvolution of high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).

Antibacterial activity of NCQDs against the eight species of bacteria on agar plates. Disks a, b, and c contained 0.5 mg of NCQDs, 0.5 mg of glucose, and 0.5 mg of DETA, respectively. Alkaline DETA was neutralized with hydrochloric acid to form neutral hydrochloride.
TEM images of MRSA (A and B) and E. coli (C and D) with the treatment of normal saline (A and C as control groups) and NCQDs (B and D as experimental groups).

Disk-diffusion tests (A, C) and statistical analysis (B, D) of the effect of GSH (A, B) and GSSG (C, D) on the antibacterial activity of NCQDs. Disk a in (A) and (C) contained 0.5 mg of pure NCQDs. Disk b in (A) contained 0.5 mg of GSH and 0.5 mg of NCQDs; Disk b in (C) contained 0.5 mg of GSSG and 0.5 mg of NCQDs. Error bars represented the standard deviation of three parallel measurements. P < 0.01 indicated a significant difference and was performed by t-test.
Photographs of healing process of the wound infected with MRSA in control group treated with normal saline, NCQDs group, and vancomycin group.

WBC count (A) and CRP level (B) of the experimental rats; (C) H&E-stained histological sections of the middle tissues of the wound, including infection for 48 h and treatment for 14 days; Scale bars equal to 200 μm. Black arrows indicate the blood vessels, and red arrows indicate the connective tissue. Error bars represented the standard deviation of six parallel measurements. ** (p < 0.01) represented a significant difference and was performed by t-test (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as an antimicrobial agent against Staphylococcus for the treatment of infected wounds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Fujian Medical University Union Hospital, Fuzhou 350001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, 1 North Xuefu Road, University Town, Fuzhou, Fujian 350122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antimicrobial resistance is becoming more and more serious and has become a potential hazard to human life and health. The fabrication of some new antibacterial substances against resistant bacteria is demanded. With the wide application and research of carbon nanomaterials, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were synthesized by a one-step chemical route herein. The particle size of NCQDs in the range of 2–5 nm were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy, and dynamic light scattering. The functional groups and optical properties of NCQDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Disk-diffusion tests showed that the NCQDs had specific antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus. TEM showed that the NCQDs could destroy the cell structure of Staphylococcus aureus and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) but could not combat Escherichia coli. The antibacterial mechanism may be that positively charged NCQDs firstly interacted with the negatively charged bacteria, and then specifically anchored on some specific sites on the surface of Staphylococcus. The NCQDs were applied to treat wounds infected with MRSA and showed the same therapeutic effect as vancomycin. Photomicrographs of hematoxylin-eosin-stained histological sections showed that the NCQDs at concentrations effectively killing S. aureus and MRSA caused negligible toxicity to the main rat organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. Thus, the NCQDs can be developed as a promising antibacterial agent for Staphylococcus. And the NCQDs are likely to treat local infections caused by Staphylococcus clinically, especially S. aureus and MRSA.

ce:section.ce:para: For more than six decades, antibacterial drugs have been considered a panacea for infections. However, as early as 1945, Alexander Fleming in his Nobel Lecture warned about bacterial resistance to penicillin [1]. With the vast and arbitrary use of antibacterial drugs in recent years, antimicrobial resistance (AMR) has extensively occurred and become increasingly serious. In 2014, a global report about antibiotic resistance from the World Health Organization (WHO) revealed that AMR exists worldwide and can affect anyone, of any age, in any country [2]. Conversely, novel antibacterial drugs have been discovered since 1990 [3]. Increasing events of AMR induced the emergence of multidrug-resistant bacteria. Moreover, increasingly growing drug-resistant bacteria are elevating common infections and minor injuries into a life-threatening disease.

Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is a common Gram-positive opportunistic pathogen that can cause a variety of diseases, including skin infections, sepsis, pneumonia, and bloodstream infections. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), discovered in 1961, is resistant to common β-lactam antibiotics, including cephalosporins and carbapenems [4]. Due to the serious abuse of antibacterial drugs, MRSA has become an important pathogen in nosocomial and community-associated infections. Nowadays, the increasing prevalence and horizontal transmission of MRSA in hospitals and communities pose a considerable clinical challenge to the management of severe infections worldwide [5]. Furthermore, MRSA has been associated with significant morbidity and mortality since 1961 and emerged as a critical cause of skin, soft tissue, wound, and surgical site infections [6]. Thus, the design and exploitation of new antibacterial drugs against MRSA are important for clinical researchers and pharmacologists.

At present, vancomycin is the gold standard for the treatment for serious MRSA infections. However, MRSA is becoming resistant to glycopeptides, and vancomycin resistance has become evident in select clinical settings with rising minimal inhibitory concentrations (MIC), growing awareness of heteroresistance, and emergence of intermediate-resistant and fully resistant strains [5]. In addition, Gu et al. reported that Staphylococcus epidermidis comprises 76.4% of linezolid-resistance coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (LRCoNS) appearing in Europe, America, and Asia [7]. Thus, new antibacterial agents with improved activity and low toxicity against Staphylococcus, especially MRSA, need to be developed.

Nanoparticle-based antibacterial agents such as carbon nanomaterials have been extensively developed in recent years. Carbon nanomaterials, including graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have attracted increasing attention because of their antimicrobial properties [8–14]. Hu et al. first reported that GO and rGO nanosheets can effectively inhibit the growth of Escherichia coli (E. coli) [10]. Subsequently, Zou et al. found that wrinkled GO with a roughness grade of ˜500 nm exerts a strong antibacterial effect against E. coli and S. aureus and that wrinkled GO with a roughness grade of ˜845 nm shows effective antibacterial activity against Mycobacterium smegmatis [11]. Li et al. demonstrated that hydrated GO with high
C density exerts strong antibacterial effects on E. coli through membrane binding and induction of lipid peroxidation [12]. Luo et al. proved that adenine-modified GQDs exhibit good antibacterial activity against E. coli with the help of white light of irradiation [13]. In addition, Zardini et al. confirmed that multi-walled carbon nanotubes functionalized by arginine and lysine under microwave radiation are specifically effective against Gram-negative bacteria (e.g., E. coli and Salmonella typhimurium) [14]. Overall, carbon nanomaterials provide an excellent prospect for the development of antibacterial agents because of their controlled size and easy functionalization. However, the design and development of antibiotics based on carbon nanomaterials and metal-free nanoparticles are still at the primary stage. In addition, carbon nanomaterials for MRSA are extremely lacking. Thus, carbon nanomaterials with excellent and specific antibacterial activity should be developed for future clinical applications.

The unique properties of CQDs allow the modulation of the doping degree of different elements and the modification of functional groups [15,16]. Thus, CQDs doped with different elements are widely studied and used in medical biology, including gene detection [17], drug analysis [18,19], in vitro bioimaging [20,21], bacterial labeling [22]. Yang et al. synthesized quaternized carbon dots (CDs) with the double properties of antibacterial activity and bacterial identification ability through a simple carboxyl-amine reaction between lauryl betaine and amine-functionalized CDs [23]. Quaternized CDs demonstrate an antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria, including S. aureus, Micrococcus luteus, and Bacillus subtilis, but their antibacterial activity toward MRSA is unknown.

In this work, in order to develop some effective antimicrobial substrates against resistant bacteria, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) were designed and synthesized by a simple one-step chemical route. As shown in Fig. 1 , glucose and diethylenetriamine were used as the cheap and friendly synthetic material to prepare the NCQDs by heat fusion method. The prepared NCQDs exhibited antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus, especially against MRSA. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) showed that the NCQDs could destroy the cell structure of S. aureus and MRSA but could not influence the activity of E. coli. Reduced glutathione could enhance the antibacterial effect of NCQDs against Staphylococcus, while oxidized glutathione did not affect its antibacterial activity, showing distinctive antibacterial properties. The NCQDs were applied to treat the wounds infected with MRSA, which showed the same therapeutic effect as vancomycin. Furthermore, in vivo toxicity tests showed that the NCQDs at concentrations effectively killing S. aureus and MRSA caused negligible toxicity to the main rat organs in vivo, including heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. The NCQDs, which had the feature of simple preparation, easy synthesis, and low cost, could be developed as an alternative for the treatment of the local tissue infection of Staphylococcus.

d(+)-glucose monohydrate, diethylenetriamine (DETA), absolute ethanol, and chloral hydrate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai China). Reduced glutathione (GSH) and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai China). The Amicon Ultra 4 ml centrifugal filter with a membrane NMWL of 3 kDa was from Millipore (USA). Mueller-Hinton (MH) broth, Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and Mueller-Hinton Agar (MHA) were purchased from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (Qingdao China). Columbia blood agar medium (blood agar plate) was from Jiangmen Caring Trading Co., Ltd (Jiangmen China). Sterile wound dressing (6 cm × 7 cm) was purchased from Qingdao Hainuo Biological Engineering Co., Ltd (Qingdao China). Medical reticular elastic bandage (5 #) was purchased from Healthy Sanitation Kingdom Medical Apparatus Co., Ltd (Foshan China). Polystyrene round-bottom tube (5 mL) was purchased from Corning Science México S.A. de C.V. (México). Vancomycin (trade name: Vancocin) was from VIANEX S.A. (PLANT C) (Greece). Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC6538), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC43300), Staphylococcus epidermidis (ATCC12228), Escherichia coli (ATCC25922), Salmonella paratyphi-β (CMCC(B)50094), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CMCC(B)10104) and Enterococcus faecalis (ATCC29212) were purchased from Shanghai Luwei Microbial Sci. & Tech. Co. Ltd (Shanghai China). The standard strain of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus is ATCC43300. Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm; EMD Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was used in all experiments.

According to the previous report with some modification [18], the NCQDs were synthesized from d(+)-Glucose monohydrate and DETA. The specific process was as follows. Two g glucose was firstly liquated at 150 ℃ for 20 min in 50 mL of the round bottom flask. After the addition of 1 mL DETA, the reaction was kept at 150 °C for another 1 h. Then, the black solid was obtained. The 0.2 g black solid was dissolved in deionized water, and then the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 by hydrochloric acid and diluted with deionized water to 4 mL. The above solution was placed in the Amicon Ultra centrifugal filter and centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 30 min. Subsequently, the filtrate was discarded and 2 mL of deionized water was added and centrifuged at 7500 rpm for 30 min again. The process was repeated 5 times. And then, the upper liquid was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the NCQDs powder. The synthesis yield of the NCQDs is calculated to be 14.97%.

The MRSA was obtained from the Department of Laboratory Medicine in the 476 Hospital of PLA. After all bacteria in the experiment were first cultured on Columbia blood agar medium at 35 ℃ for 24 h in a bacteriological incubator, a single colony was inoculated into sterile saline solution (0.9% NaCl) to get the bacterial suspension. The concentration of bacterial suspension was controlled at 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL by measuring the optical density at a wavelength of 600 nm (OD600), and the OD600 value of bacterial suspension was about 0.1 [24]. The bacterial suspension with 0.1 of OD600 value was directly used for a series of subsequent tests, or diluted to different concentrations for other subsequent tests.

Disk-diffusion method [25]: Bacterial suspension with 0.1 of OD600 value was coated on the MHA plate (Φ = 90 mm) by using medical cotton swabs. Six disks were placed on the plate, and the center of the disks was less than 24 mm. The plates were placed in a biochemical incubator with 35 ℃ for 18 h. Each zone diameter was clearly measurable and could be judged by the naked eyes. The complete zone diameter of the disk was measured above a black background illuminated with reflected light. Each antibacterial test of disk-diffusion method was performed independently for three times, and each test was performed in triplicate.

Broth dilution method [26]: 13 sterile tubes (12 × 75 mm) were positioned in a row. The 1.6 mL of MH broth and 0.4 mL of 5.12 mg/mL NCQDs were added into the first tube and then mixed. Then, 1 mL of MH broth was added into the 12 remaining tubes. The 1 mL of the mixture in the first tube was moved into the second tube and then mixed. Then, 1 mL of the mixture in the second tube was move into the third tube. This process was repeated until the eleventh tube. Then, 1 mL of the mixture in the eleventh tube was discarded, and the twelfth tube without NCQDs served as the control. Each tube was added with 1 mL of 1.5 × 106 CFU/mL bacterial suspension. After these tubes were placed in a bacteriological incubator with 35 ℃ for 18 h, the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was determined by the naked eyes under the condition of directional light. MIC test of broth dilution method was performed independently for three times, and each test was performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM characterization of NCQDs against bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: The 20 μL of 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL bacterial suspension was inoculated into 2480 μL of LB broth and incubated at 35 ℃ for 6 h. Subsequently, 500 μL of 5.12 mg/mL NCQD solution was added into the above broth and then incubated 35 ℃ for 12 h. Then, the broth was centrifuged for 30 min at 3000 rpm, and the supernatant was discarded. The preparation of the bacteria for TEM characterization was carried out according to the reported method [27]. The condensed bacteria were mixed with 2 mL of 2.5% glutaraldehyde and fixed for 2 h at room temperature. After centrifugation, the bacteria were mixed with 1 mL of 1% osmic acid and post-fixed for 2 h at room temperature. Afterward, the bacteria were dehydrated through a concentration series of ethanol (30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100%) for 15 min and then embedded in Epon/Araldite resin at 60 ℃ for 12 h. The samples were cut into ultrathin sections (60–80 nm thick) with a microtome equipped with a diamond knife and then stained with 4% uranyl acetate and 0.2% lead citrate in the dark. After drying, the sections were observed by TEM (Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viability of HeLa and PANC-1 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assays [28] were used to test the effect of the prepared NCQDs on the viability of HeLa and PANC-1 cells. The 300 μL of the cells solution were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well. Cells were further incubated with the NCQDs at the indicated concentrations for 24 h. After the culture medium was removed from each well, 300 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well and cultured at 37 ℃ for 4 h. After that, the MTT solution was replaced with 300 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), followed by shaking for 15 min. The absorbance was measured at 490 nm on a microplate reader with Skanlt Software 3.2 (Multiskan GO, Thermo Scientific).

Animal experiments were performed in accordance with the Guideline for Animal Experimentation with the approval of the Animal Care and Use Committee of Fujian Medical University (No. FJMU IACUC 2018-083). Sprague–Dawley (SD) rats with a body weight of 250 ± 20 g were employed in the experiments. A total of 18 SD rats were randomly divided into three groups: control group, NCQDs group, and vancomycin group. After the SD rats were anesthetized with 10% chloral hydrate solution (applying 0.3 mL/100 g) by intraperitoneal injection, the dorsal hair of the SD rats was shaved with a hair clipper and a nummular full-thickness skin was resected with a scalpel [29]. The wound was about 1.8 cm in diameter, as shown in Fig. S1. The operation was performed under aseptic conditions before the infection. The sterile wound dressing added with 2 mL of 1.5 × 108 CFU/mL MRSA suspension was adhered to the nummular wound at the back of the SD rats and was fastened with medical reticular elastic bandage. Then, the rats were separately raised in cages at a standardized temperature. After 48 h, the nummular wound was infected with MRSA and the wound dressing with bacteria was discarded. The wounds were observed and photographed before and after infection.

The control group as negative control was treated with sterile wound dressing containing 2 mL of normal saline. NCQDs group as experimental group and vancomycin group as positive control were treated with sterile wound dressing containing 2 ml of 2.5 mg/mL NCQDs solution and the same volume of 0.25 mg/mL vancomycin solution, respectively. The wound dressings were changed once a day in the period of 14 days. On the 4th, 7th and 14th day from the start of treatment, the wounds were investigated, compared and photographed. White blood cells (WBC) and C-reactive proteins (CRP) were measured by tail venous blood of all the rats in the experiment. On the 14th day, all the rats were sacrificed, and the tissues of wounds were harvested. The obtained tissues of all the wound were homogenized in sterile normal saline to obtain the homogenized tissues which were diluted and placed on Columbia blood agar medium, determining the amount of MRSA in the tissues of the three groups [30].

The wound tissues infected with MRSA for 48 h and treated with normal saline, NCQDs, and vancomycin for 14 days were collected and stored in 10% formalin to prepare pathological slides for histological analysis. After 5 mL of 5 mg/mL NCQDs solution was used for the continuous gavage for 7 days, the rats were injected with excess of 10% chloral hydrate and then sacrificed. The heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of the rats were collected and then stored in 10% formalin to prepare pathological slides. After staining with H&E [29], histological images were acquired on an optical microscope (Nikon Eclipse E100, Japan).

The size and height of NCQDs were characterized by TEM, atomic force microscopy (AFM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). TEM was used to analyze the size distribution and crystal lattice spacing of the prepared NCQDs. Fig. 2 A directly showed that the quasi-circular NCQDs with favorable dispersion had an average diameter of about 5 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 2B revealed that the crystal lattice spacing of NCQDs is 0.22 nm, which was ascribed to the diffraction facets of graphite carbon. The height distribution of the prepared NCQDs was investigated by AFM. Fig. 2D was the height profile along the lines of AFM in Fig. 2C. As shown in Fig. 2C and D, the NCQDs exhibited a uniform height of 2–4 nm. In addition, the distribution of NCQDs obtained from DLS showed that the particle size distribution of NCQDs was in the range from 2.8 nm to 5 nm (Fig. S2), which was in accord with the TEM results. According to the above results, it can be determined that the particle size range of NCQDs was between 2 and 5 nm.

The chemical states, functional groups, and optical property of NCQDs were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. S3A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum illustrated that NCQDs had the strong terminal absorption ascribed to the π → π* transition of conjugated CC bands in the range of 200 to 280 nm, and an absorption peak ascribed to the n → π* transition of conjugated CO bands at 349 nm [31,32]. Furthermore, the NCQDs exhibited fluorescence emission property centered at 455 nm when excited with 335 nm (Fig. S3B). Fig. 3 showed the XPS results of NCQDs. The survey spectrum in Fig. 3A showed the peaks of C1s at 284.8 eV, N1s at 399.2 eV and O1s at 531.2 eV, indicating that NCQDs were mainly composed of C, N and O with the successful doping of N [13]. And the atomic percentages of C, N and O were 68.89%, 15.27% and 15.84%, respectively, which was similar to the quantitative results of C (65.92%), H (3.34%), N (15.80%) and O (14.94%) obtained from elemental analysis. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s in Fig. 3B revealed four peaks at 284.7, 285.5, 286.2 and 287.3 eV, corresponding to CC/CC (graphitic carbon), CN (sp3 carbon), CO (sp3 carbon) and CO (carbonyl carbon), respectively [17,33,34]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s in Fig. 3C exhibited two peaks at 398.9 and 399.9 eV, corresponding to the bonding of CNC (pyridinic nitrogen) and CNH (pyrrolic nitrogen), respectively [35], which confirmed the doping effect of N in NCQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3D) suggested three bonding peaks of OH (hydroxyl oxygen) at 530.6, *OCO (carboxyl oxygen) at 531.2 and CO (sp2 oxygen) at 531.9 eV [36]. Moreover, FTIR spectrum in Fig. S4 was in accorded with the analysis of XPS, that NCQDs had the following functional groups, including −OH/NH (3425 cm−1), CH (2926 cm−1, 2854 cm−1), CC (1637 cm−1), CH2 (1491 cm−1, 1439 cm−1), CN (1344 cm−1, 1237 cm−1, 1140 cm−1, 1078 cm−1) and NH (755 cm−1, 567 cm−1) [14,37,38]. The results of XPS and FTIR indicated that the introduction of DETA in the preparing process of NCQDs could successfully dope N atoms into NCQDs mainly through the covalent binding of CN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of NCQDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The disk-diffusion method was applied to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the NCQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 . Three disks on agar plates contained NCQDs (disk a), glucose (disk b), and DETA (disk c). S. aureus (ATCC6538), S. aureus (ATCC43300), S. epidermidis, MRSA, E. coli, Salmonella paratyphi-β (S. paratyphi-β), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), and Enterococcus faecalis (E. faecalis) were selected as the model organisms. Obvious inhibition zones (disk a) were observed on the agar plates incubating S. aureus (ATCC6538), S. aureus (ATCC43300), S. epidermidis, and MRSA. The inhibition zones on the agar plates incubating S. aureus (ATCC43300) were 15.5 mm on average, which was larger than the ˜14.5 mm diameter in the plates incubating S. aureus (ATCC6538), S. epidermidis, and MRSA. However, no inhibition zones were observed on the agar plates incubating E. coli, S. paratyphi-β, P. aeruginosa, and E. faecalis, implying that the NCQDs exerted negligible antibacterial effects on these species. To confirm the antibacterial effect of the NCQDs and exclude the possible effect of synthetic substrate on their antimicrobial activity, we also evaluated the antimicrobial activity of DETA and glucose as control groups. No inhibition zone was found around the disk containing glucose (disk b) and DETA (disk c), suggesting that the equal amounts synthetic substrates demonstrated no antibacterial effect on the eight species of bacteria. The above experimental results presented that NCQDs exerted specific antimicrobial activity on Gram-positive Staphylococcus. Furthermore, the MIC of the NCQDs on MRSA and S. aureus (ATCC6538) were measured to be 0.128 and 0.256 mg/mL through the broth dilution method, respectively.

The possible destructive effect of the NCQDs on the different bacteria was further investigated via TEM to understand their antibacterial effect (Fig. 5 ). MRSA and E. coli treated with normal saline were served as the negative control groups. As shown in Fig. 5A and C, MRSA and E. coli treated with normal saline showed an intact cell structure with no observable cell death or damage. In contrast, the cells of MRSA treated with NCQDs showed obvious rupture and loss of integrity (Fig. 5B). Similarly, S. aureus (ATCC6538) was also disintegrated when exposed to the same concentration of NCQDs (Fig. S5). The cell structure collapse of MRSA and S. aureus should be due to the destroy of the cytoplasmic membrane by the introduction of NCQDs [39]. Meanwhile, when both MRSA and S. aureus were treated with NCQDs, the intracellular densities decreased obviously, suggesting the loss of some intracellular substance (Figs. 5B and S5). In sharp contrast, the cell morphology of E. coli treated with the same concentration NCQDs in Fig. 5C were almost the same as the control group in Fig. 5D, suggesting almost no antimicrobial activity of NCQDs against E. coli. Thus, the prepared NCQDs showed an antibacterial activity specific against S. aureus (ATCC6538) and MRSA, which inspired the investigation of the antibacterial mechanism of NCQDs.

It is reported that nitrogen- or oxygen-doped carbon nanomaterials, including GQDs and CQDs, can destroy the bacterial cells through the generation of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of H2O2 or O2 molecules [40,41]. In this work, the effect of glutathione (GSH) as antioxidant and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) on the antibacterial activity of the NCQDs was investigated to probe the possible antibacterial mechanism. GSH, a tripeptide consisting of glycine, cysteine, and glutamic acid, prevents the noxious effect of ROS in important cellular components [42]. The sulfhydryl group of GSH donates a reducing equivalent (H++e−) to other molecules, including ROS. By donating an electron, GSH itself becomes reactive and readily reacts with another reactive GSH to form glutathione disulfide (GSSG). Fig. 6 showed the effect of GSH on the antibacterial activity of the NCQDs. As shown in Fig. 6A, disk b containing the mixture of GSH and NCQDs had larger inhibition zones than disk a with pure NCQDs on the agar plates incubating S. aureus (ATCC6538), S. aureus (ATCC43300), S. epidermidis, and MRSA, suggesting that GSH could enhance the antibacterial effect of NCQDs against Staphylococcus. As depicted in Fig. 6B, the diameters of the inhibition zones of disk b and disk a differed significantly. However, the NCQDs remained ineffective against E. coli, S. paratyphi-β, P. aeruginosa, and E. faecalis even after the addition of GSH, as shown in Fig. S6. In addition, Fig. 6C showed that the diameters of disk b with the mixture of GSSG and NCQDs were almost the same as those of disk a with pure NCQDs on the agar plates incubating the same four Staphylococcus species. This result implies that GSSG did not affect the antibacterial effect of the NCQDs on Staphylococcus. Furthermore, the inhibition zone diameters of disk b and disk a showed no significant difference, as shown in Fig. 6D. Moreover, GSSG did not change the fact that the NCQDs were ineffective against E. coli, S. paratyphi-β, P. aeruginosa, and E. faecalis, as shown in Fig. S7. In order to monitor whether or not ROS were generated by NCQDs during killing bacterial, DCFH-DA assays were carried out. Fig. S8 showed that there was almost no fluorescence in the S. aureus (ATCC6538) solution containing DCFH-DA by adding either NCQDs (curve a) or the mixture of NCQDs and GSH (curve b). After 20 μL of 1% H2O2 was added into the above mixture, the fluorescence signal appeared apparently, as shown in curve c and d of Fig. S8. According to the above results, we can preliminarily conclude that GSH enhanced the antibacterial effect of the NCQDs through its reductibility from sulfhydryl group. In addition, the enhancement of GSH on the antibacterial activity of the NCQDs suggested that NCQDs or the mixture of NCQDs and GSH didn’t produce ROS in the process of combatting bacteria. And the underlying mechanism was different from the simple ROS effect.

Jian et al. synthesized super-cationic CQDs with a highly positive charge (zeta potential of +45 mV) and demonstrated that the surface charge of the CQDs was an important factor in determining the antibacterial mechanism [39]. In terms of the influencing factor of the surface potential of the NCQDs, the zeta potentials of the NCQDs with 5 and 50 mg/mL were +23 and +15 mV, respectively, as shown in Fig. S9. The surface potentials of E. coli and S. aureus (ATCC6538) were measured to be −19.3 and −15.7 mV, respectively. In other words, the surface of the bacteria was negatively charged. As shown in Fig. 4, the NCQDs were only effective against Staphylococcus and exhibited no antibacterial activity against other bacteria. In addition, GSH can enhance the antibacterial activity of the NCQDs against Staphylococcus (Fig. 6A). However, GSH and GSSG did not significantly affect the surface potential of the NCQDs (Fig. S9), indicating that GSH and GSSG did not affect the antibacterial activity of the NCQDs by surface charge. Furthermore, the fluorescent images in Fig. S10 showed that NCQDs could bind and image S. aureus (ATCC6538) blue under the excitation of ultraviolet light, but not for E. coli. So, we can infer that positively charged NCQDs exhibited specific antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus by first interacting with the negatively charged bacteria and then specifically anchoring on some specific sites on the surface of Staphylococcus. It is known that Gram-positive bacteria (like S. aureus in this work) and Gram-negative bacteria (like E. coli in this work) have different chemical composition of their cell walls [43]. S. aureus and its related bacteria had porous cell wall due to the distributed thick layer of cross-linked peptidoglycan on plasma membrane. The porous cell walls may promote the interaction between NCQDs and S. aureus and MRSA, and then play a bactericidal action [44]. However, the cell wall of E. coli contains an outer membrane and intermittently peptidoglycan network, which may cause relative weak interaction between NCQDs and E. coli. The different anchoring effect of NCQDs and bacteria may induce variable antibacterial effect. However, the current investigation of the antibacterial mechanism of the NCQDs was in the initial phase. Thus, further and thorough explorations are needed and underway.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic effect of NCQDs on infected wound in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of the NCQDs was evaluated in vivo. A nummular full-thickness skin at the back of the SD rats was resected with a scalpel and infected with MRSA. NCQDs group as experimental group, vancomycin as positive control group, and normal saline as negative control group were employed to heal the infected skin wounds, respectively. Fig. 7 showed the healing process of the infected wound. Fresh postoperative wound with a diameter of about 1.8 cm was infected with MRSA. After infection for 48 h, the abscess appeared on the surface of the infected wounds. Then, the infected wounds were treated with normal saline, NCQDs, and vancomycin. After 4 days, scab appeared on the infected wounds treated with NCQDs and vancomycin, whereas pus and exudates were observed on the wound of the normal saline group. After treatment for 7 days, the surface of the wound still contained pus and exudates in the negative control group. Compared with that in the negative control group, the size of the infected wounds decreased significantly in the NCQD and vancomycin groups. After treatment for 14 days, the wound areas in the negative control, NCQD and vancomycin groups were counted to be 26.8%, 0.5% and 0.2% of the wound areas after initial 48 h of infection, respectively. In other words, the wounds were essentially closed in the two treatment group, implying that the infected wounds were healed with the treatment of NCQD or vancomycin. Nevertheless, the wounds in the negative control group were still as large as those in the NCQDs and vancomycin groups at 7 days, and exudates remained on the surface of the infected wounds in the negative control group. Meanwhile, the tissues of infected wounds were taken out to quantitate the amount of MRSA, and the results was shown in Fig. S11. A number of MRSA colonies appeared in the negative control group, but there was no grown colony in the NCQDs and vancomycin groups. These results indicated that the NCQDs were as effective as vancomycin in treating skin lesions with MRSA, killed MRSA in the sites of the infected wounds, and shortened healing time of the infected wound. Moreover, the NCQDs were compared with other carbon quantum dots with antibacterial activities in terms of synthesis substrates, preparation steps, involved bacteria, and animal model, as shown in Table S1. It showed that the NCQDs possessed therapeutic effect for MRSA infected-wounds accompanied with the virtues of simple synthetic procedure for the NCQDs with cheap raw materials.

White blood cells (WBCs) and C-reactive proteins (CRPs) in rats were also monitored throughout the study. Fig. 8 A and B showed the changes in WBC and CRP of the different groups in the experiment. The levels of WBC and CRP in the three groups were low and similar before the wound was infected. After the wounds infected with MRSA for 48 h, the levels of WBC and CRP in the three groups increased significantly, suggesting successful infection. After starting treatment, the levels of WBC and CRP started to decline within 24 h in the NCQD and vancomycin groups. However, the levels of WBC and CRP did not decrease within 24 h in the negative control group. After continuous treatment, the levels of WBC and CRP decreased persistently in the NCQD and vancomycin groups. On 14th day, the two levels approached the pre-infection levels in the two treated groups. However, in the same process, the levels of WBC and CRP decreased very slowly in the normal saline group and were still obviously higher than the pre-infection levels and those of the two other groups on the 14th day. The results suggested that the NCQDs, similar to vancomycin, can reduce the levels of WBC and CRP in the rats due to the effective treatment towards MRSA infected wound.

H&E staining of the middle tissues of the wound was carried out after 48 h of infection and after 14 days of treatment. Fig. 8C shows the photomicrographs of H&E-stained histological sections. After the wound infected for 48 h, many neutrophils appeared in the tissue. This phenomenon was consistent with the elevated levels of WBC and CRP in the serum, indicating that the wound was infected by MRSA. Meanwhile, the capillaries in the tissue of the infected wound thickened and the number of red blood cells in the blood vessels increased, as shown by the black arrow in Fig. 8C, implying a certain amount of edema in the infected wound. After 14 days of treatment, the inflammation in the tissues basically disappeared in the NCQDs and vancomycin groups, and dense connective tissue was observed on the surface of the wound, as shown by the red arrow in Fig. 8C. However, after 14 days, the inflammation in the normal saline group was similar to that after 48 h of MRSA infection. Compared with those in the negative and positive control groups, the wound infected by MRSA was healed in the NCQD group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity test of NCQDs in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the prepared NCQDs to mammalian cells was investigated by evaluating cell viability. Fig. S12 showed the viabilities of HeLa and PANC-1 cells cultured with different concentrations of NCQDs for 24 h in MTT assays [28]. The viabilities of HeLa and PANC-1 cells were more than 80% when the concentration of NCQDs was lower than 0.128 mg/mL. However, the cell viabilities were less than 80% at 0.128 mg/mL with the continued decline to the increased NCQDs after 24 h incubation. These results implied that high concentrations of NCQDs had certain toxicity to the two cell lines in vitro.

The toxicity of the NCQDs in vivo was evaluated by the pathomorphology of the main organs of rats in the study. Fig. S13 showed the photomicrographs of H&E-stained histological sections of heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney. The tissues from the five main organs in the rats treated with NCQDs maintained an undisturbed organizational structure as compared to those from the control group treated normal saline. The result means two possibilities: (1) the NCQDs do not produce toxic side effects on the main organs of organisms; (2) the NCQDs can be effectively converted into non-toxic substances in the body. Of course, in vivo toxicity of NCQDs is something that needs to be studied in detail. Based on the above results, it is preliminary to infer the NCQDs may be a promising carbon nanomaterial with negligible toxicity in vivo on the main organs.

The NCQDs with the size range of 2–5 nm were synthesized through a simple chemical route. Compared with other carbon nanomaterial, the NCQDs had the specific antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus, including S. aureus (ATCC6538), S. aureus (ATCC43300), S. epidermidis, and MRSA. Distinctively, GSH could enhance the antibacterial effect of NCQDs against Staphylococcus, while GSSG did not affect its antibacterial activity. We preliminarily concluded that the specific antimicrobial activity was due to the fact that the NCQDs firstly interacted with negatively charged bacteria and then specifically anchored to the specific sites on the surface of Staphylococcus to cause the rupture and death of bacterial cells. The NCQDs exhibited the same therapeutic effect as vancomycin in treating the wound infected with MRSA. In vitro and in vivo toxicity test revealed that the NCQDs had acceptable biocompatibility. Meanwhile, the NCQDs effectively killing MRSA in infected-wound had the feature of a simply prepared step from cheap synthetic substrates. This work provides a simple strategy for the preparation of carbon quantum dots with specific antibacterial activity, and also expands a new direction for the application of carbon quantum dots in the medical field. Of course, it should be admitted that the MIC of NCQDs still needs to be improved, which is conducting on the present and future. In addition, the antibacterial mechanism of carbon quantum dots also needs exploration in the further study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (81703477), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology in Fujian Province (2017Y9121) and University Distinguished Young Scientific Research Talent Training Program of Fujian Province (2018). The authors thank the Clinical Laboratory in the 476 Hospital of PLA for the supply and antibacterial test of NCQDs against MRSA strain.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2019.03.042.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
